# Representing Architecture State and Consequences {#sec-data-representations-world-models}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The limits of my language mean the limits of my world."*
>
> — Ludwig Wittgenstein, *Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus* (1922)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** The philosopher Ludwig Wittgenstein argued that language constrains what we can conceive. The architectural version is narrower. An AI method can change only the design state exposed to it, and a reviewer can examine only evidence that remains connected to that state. If an architecture description has no way to express a coherence assumption, for example, a generator may mention it but a tool cannot check how the candidate handles it.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Before an AI method can change an architecture, it needs an explicit account of the current design, the proposed change, and the conditions that change must satisfy. That representation must connect versions, actions, anticipated effects, model predictions, tool observations at several fidelities, costs, provenance, failed runs, rejected alternatives, decisions, and their owners. A scoped consequence model predicts or constrains what may follow from a proposed action. A separate replay package contains the inputs and environment needed to rerun the evaluation.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must be represented about a design, a proposed change, and its consequences
so AI can act and another architect can trace the result?
:::

Suppose an AI optimizer proposes a larger L2 cache and a simulator reports fewer
misses. An architect still cannot judge the proposal without knowing which
workload ran, which cache parameters changed, which simulator configuration
produced the result, and how the candidate compares with the baseline. A lower
miss count under a different prefetcher or workload slice does not establish
that the larger cache helped.

@sec-architecture-20-ontology defined a bounded architecture study through its
question, comparison, evidence, and intended decision. To carry out the L2
comparison, the current design state and every proposed change must be explicit.
The representation records the current state, the proposed change, the
effect the architect or AI method anticipates, any model prediction used, the
observation returned by the tool, and the decision that followed. A scoped
model may predict or constrain the
consequences of the change, but it does not replace the record of what actually
ran. If a workload choice, coherence assumption, or rejected candidate remains
hidden in a script or in an architect's memory, the AI method cannot reliably
account for it and another architect cannot reconstruct the result.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can identify the design state an AI method needs and the
records another architect needs to trace its result. Specifically, you can

- **Explain** why papers, repositories, and benchmark results omit design state that AI-assisted work needs;
- **Connect** a design version, proposed action, anticipated effect, model prediction, tool observation, decision, and decision owner in one traceable trajectory;
- **Record** the cost, fidelity, scope, and provenance of each observation;
- **Spot** undocumented assumptions, failed runs, and rejected alternatives that could mislead later action;
- **Distinguish** represented state from a scoped model that predicts or constrains the consequences of a proposed change.
:::

## What an Architecture Representation Must Connect

An architecture representation connects information that final artifacts often
separate. It does not need to be one monolithic file. It may span a
configuration schema, workload manifest, candidate table, run log, review
record, and version-control history, provided those artifacts refer to the same
design versions and proposed changes.

> **Architecture representation.** An architecture representation records the
> design state an AI method can read, compare, and propose changing, together
> with enough history for another architect to inspect the result. It provides
> three related views.
>
> - **Current-state view:** The workload, design state, constraints, and fields available at one version.
> - **Trajectory view:** The proposed actions, anticipated effects, model predictions, tool observations, failed runs, rejected alternatives, and decisions that produced that version.
> - **Context view:** The assumptions, uncertainty, cost, fidelity, provenance, tool versions, and other conditions needed to interpret the state and trajectory.

@fig-trajectory-record shows how these views connect over time. Begin at design
state V1. The record identifies a proposed action and the consequence the
architect or AI method anticipates. Evaluation produces an observation with its
cost, fidelity, and provenance. If the decision owner accepts the change, the
decision advances the design to V2. A rejected action remains attached to V1
with the observation and decision that stopped it. The rejected branch does not
disappear merely because it did not produce the next version.

![**A versioned trajectory records accepted and rejected work.** Each iteration links the prior design state, proposed action, anticipated effect, tool observation, cost, fidelity, provenance, and decision. A rejected candidate remains associated with the state and action that produced it, so later work can account for the failure.](images/F5a-trajectory-record){#fig-trajectory-record width="100%" fig-alt="Two-iteration trajectory diagram. From design state version one, a proposed action and anticipated effect lead to a tool observation tagged with cost, fidelity, and provenance and then to a decision that either produces design state version two or retains the rejected candidate with its failure record. A second iteration repeats the sequence from design state version two."}

The value of @fig-trajectory-record lies in the links, not in any particular
storage format. A later AI method can see which action produced an observation
and avoid retrying a known failure. A reviewer can trace a claimed improvement
back to the workload, candidate version, prediction, tool run, and decision that
advanced it. A collection of final artifacts does not preserve those links.

## The Architecture Description Defines What May Change {#sec-architecture-descriptions-as-boundary-objects}

The current-state view needs a concrete description of the design and the
fields an AI method may propose changing. That description is one part of the
broader representation. The trajectory and context views connect it to proposed
changes, tool observations, and decisions over time.

Return to the proposed L2-cache change. Capacity and associativity may live in a
simulator configuration, the workload in a benchmark manifest, and the
coherence and software assumptions in design documentation. Those artifacts
must refer to the same candidate version. The description must also distinguish
the fields that may change from the workload, coherence contract, and other
conditions that remain fixed.

The shared description serves as a *boundary object*, an object that different
communities use for their own work while still recognizing the same underlying
thing [@StarGriesemer1989BoundaryObjects]. An architect must be able to inspect
it, a tool must be able to consume it, and an AI method must be able to propose
changes without silently altering the comparison.

At minimum, an architecture description should identify the design version,
the fields that may change and their units, the fixed constraints and
invariants, and the tools that consume the description. In the L2 example, the
record might show a current capacity of 1 MiB, a proposed capacity of 2 MiB,
and an unchanged line size, prefetcher, coherence assumption, and workload
version. The environment defined in
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces applies the declared legality
checks and records whether the proposed change passes, fails, or requires
review.

The relevant fields depend on the architecture work. A memory-hierarchy
description may include cache sizes, associativity, replacement policy,
prefetching, coherence assumptions, bandwidth, latency, and workload mix. An
accelerator or compute-subsystem description may instead include supported
operations, data layout, local storage, vector width, dataflow, quantization,
compiler and runtime assumptions, and fallback behavior. A paper-reading task,
simulator-driven search, RTL-generation task, and post-silicon telemetry task
should not use identical records. They do need explicit mutable fields and
fixed invariants before an AI method proposes a change.

## Representing What a Design Change May Do {#sec-toward-architecture-world-models}

The representation records the current design, the proposed change, and what
happened to earlier candidates. Choosing the next change also requires an
estimate of what that change may do. Architects already use simulators,
analytical models, learned surrogates, design rules, and constraint checkers for
that purpose. An AI method needs their valid inputs and limits stated
explicitly.

> **Scoped consequence model.** A scoped consequence model estimates what a
> proposed change may do or evaluates it against a declared constraint. It may
> be a simulator, surrogate (a cheap learned stand-in), analytical predictor,
> rule system, or constraint model. Its record states which design states and
> proposed actions it covers, how it was calibrated, and which consequences it
> cannot evaluate.
>
> **Architecture world model.** An architecture world model predicts a next
> state or architectural consequence from the represented design and a proposed
> action. This adapts the world-model idea from reinforcement learning
> [@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels]. A constraint checker that evaluates a
> declared rule remains useful, but it does not predict what follows from an
> action that passes. The environment enforces the check and records its result.
> Architects and reviewers still judge the engineering result, and the named
> decision owner decides what happens next.

Predictive design-space exploration used configuration-based performance
models long before current generative AI systems
[@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. Return to the L2 example. The proposal may
anticipate that a larger cache will reduce misses. A predictor may estimate how
large that reduction will be. A later simulator run returns the miss count for
a named workload and configuration. The anticipated effect explains why the
change was proposed, the prediction is the model's estimate, and the
observation is what the tool returned. The trajectory keeps all three
distinct.

@tbl-representation-consequence-model-boundary distinguishes the first two
roles. A simulator configuration records the workload, flags, and candidate
parameters. The simulator uses that represented state to estimate what a cache
change or array geometry will do. Even when the configuration and simulator are
packaged together, the run record should distinguish the represented inputs
from the model or simulator that used them. A replay package serves another
purpose.
It contains the runnable inputs, environment, and expected observations needed
to repeat the run.

| **Example** | **What the representation records** | **How a consequence model uses it** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Simulator configuration | Records flags, model version, workload slice, seed, and command. | Defines the inputs and settings under which the simulator predicts behavior. |
| Candidate parameter table | Records declared choices and candidate values. | Feeds a surrogate or rule system that predicts latency, energy, area, or invalidity. |
| Constraint file | Records declared limits and assumptions. | Supplies the rule a checker evaluates before the environment records pass, fail, or review. |
| Failed run log | Records what happened, with provenance. | Can train or update a model of invalid regions and escalation conditions. |

: **Representations carry the inputs and history; consequence models estimate what a change may do.** An architecture world model predicts from represented state and a proposed action. A replayable artifact does not establish that the action will remain valid under new workloads, tools, or fidelity levels. {#tbl-representation-consequence-model-boundary tbl-colwidths="[20,35,35]"}

@fig-architecture-world-model separates a model prediction from a tool
observation. Read the top row from left to right. The representation supplies
the current design, workload, constraints, and prior results. The proposed
action records the field and new value together with the anticipated effect.
The scoped model then returns a prediction within its stated limits.

![**Prediction and observation follow different paths.** The proposal records an anticipated effect. The represented state and action feed a scoped consequence model that returns a prediction; the same state and action feed a declared tool path that returns an observation with its status, cost, fidelity, and provenance. The trajectory keeps the state, action, prediction, observation, failures, and decision connected. Replay additionally requires runnable inputs and environment details.](images/F5-architecture-world-model){#fig-architecture-world-model width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram showing represented state and a proposed action feeding a scoped consequence model and a separate declared tool path. The model returns a prediction, the tool path returns an observation, and both enter a trajectory record that retains provenance, coverage, failures, and the decision. A note says replay additionally requires runnable inputs and environment details."}

The lower path in @fig-architecture-world-model receives the same represented
state and proposed action. The environment applies the declared checks, invokes
the tool, and records either an observation or a failure with its status, cost,
fidelity, and provenance. The trajectory keeps the model prediction and tool
observation connected to the same state and action, together with coverage,
failures, and the decision that followed. A replay package separately supplies
the runnable inputs and environment details.

The distinction matters when an AI method uses a cheap predictor to rank a
thousand cache configurations. The simulator or implementation flow named in
the plan returns an observation that a reviewer may later use as evidence for a
specific claim. @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces examines the
interface that makes those actions and observations executable. When a
simulator serves as the consequence model, its output predicts the behavior of
the represented design. When the environment executes that same simulator, the
run record also preserves its returned value as a simulated observation. One
value therefore fills two roles, and the record must still identify its source
and avoid presenting it as an RTL, silicon, or deployment measurement.

The SCALE-Sim case in @sec-executed-scale-study provides a concrete example.
SCALE-Sim 3.0.0 is a cycle-accurate simulator for systolic accelerators
[@RajEtAl2025ScaleSimV3]. Here it maps a represented workload and proposed array
geometry to predicted cycle count and utilization. Because the prediction
depends on both represented state and proposed action, SCALE-Sim also fits the
architecture world-model definition used here. The language-model proposal
method and the deterministic baseline each receive four simulator runs. That
comparison becomes meaningful only after the workload, geometry limits,
simulator settings, and unsupported consequences are explicit.
@sec-executed-scale-study evaluates the proposal methods and results.
@tbl-executed-study-world-model shows exactly what SCALE-Sim sees and what its
predictions leave out.

| **World-model field** | **Executed SCALE-Sim comparison** |
| --- | --- |
| **Represented state** | Frozen three-layer general matrix-multiplication (GEMM) workload, transposed mechanism-probe workload, declared array-geometry bounds, weight-stationary dataflow, 128 KiB SRAMs, and fixed interface bandwidth. |
| **Action under evaluation** | Propose a row-by-column array geometry within a declared 1,024-processing-element budget. |
| **Anticipated effect** | The proposed nonbaseline geometries were intended to lower the local score relative to the 32 by 32 baseline. The language-model proposal also anticipated that 16 by 64 would beat 64 by 16 and that the ordering would reverse after M and N were exchanged. |
| **Predicted consequence** | For an accepted input, SCALE-Sim 3.0.0 predicts cycle count and average layer utilization for that workload-geometry pair. |
| **Scope and limits** | One simulator version, dataflow, memory configuration, workload slice, and score; the replay package records every setting and output. |
| **Unsupported consequence** | Energy, area, compiler effects, RTL feasibility, physical design, and performance outside the frozen workload are not evaluated. |
| **Controls, checks, and decision** | Equal four-run budgets and a shared baseline control the comparison. The 1 percent margin determines whether an observed score supports the outcome claim. The transposition probe checks the proposed mechanism, and replay checks the recorded computation. The named decision owner determines whether the result warrants further work or stopping. |

: **SCALE-Sim predicts the effect of a proposed action in this comparison.** It maps the represented workload and proposed geometry to performance predictions, while the record states which consequences remain outside its scope. {#tbl-executed-study-world-model tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

Architecture world models may be simulator-backed, learned, symbolic, or
hybrid. Each use must limit predictions about action consequences to a stated
scope. The environment in @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces
enforces the declared input and legality checks, then records the observation
or failure returned by the tool. The world model predicts what an action that
passes those checks may do within its stated scope.

Confidence in the model depends on calibration evidence within its declared
scope. Distribution shift[^fn-ml-distribution-shift], new
workloads, different software stacks, tool changes, or stronger evaluation can
place a proposed state or action outside the range covered by that calibration.
Architects should report enough scope and calibration for reviewers to judge
the prediction.

The SCALE-Sim runs returned cycle and utilization observations for the stated
performance scope. When related to the frozen claims and comparison controls,
those observations became evidence for a null architecture result and a tie
between the two proposal methods. No nonbaseline candidate cleared the
preregistered improvement margin, and the matched transposition probe
contradicted the proposed explanation. The named decision owner may use that
evidence to retain the baseline or authorize further work; neither proposal
method nor SCALE-Sim makes that decision. The experiments did not evaluate
energy, area, compiler behavior, RTL, or physical design. The tie, null result,
and rejected explanation remained in the trajectory alongside the model and its
stated limits.

[^fn-ml-distribution-shift]: Distribution shift occurs when the data a model meets in use differ from the data used to fit or calibrate it, which can reduce accuracy [@QuinoneroCandelaEtAl2009DatasetShift]. The trajectory therefore records the workloads, design region, and other conditions used for calibration.

## What Public Architecture Artifacts Leave Out

Architecture offers papers, textbooks, manuals, open-source tools, benchmark
descriptions, and selected design artifacts. These sources usually preserve
endpoints, such as a paper's winning design or a repository's latest RTL. They
rarely preserve the path to that endpoint, including simulator settings,
excluded workloads, proposed changes, failed candidates, and review decisions.

An AI method acting on a design needs those links. Public text can teach
architectural concepts and summarize reported results, but it cannot recover a
workload revision, tool option, or rejected candidate that was never recorded.
Nor are there many public records that connect each design version to what was
tried, what the tools returned, and what the architect decided. Those
connections, rather than the volume of available text, determine whether the
artifacts can support action and review.

Workload traces may be proprietary or too large to share. Simulator
configurations may live in scripts, and EDA reports may depend on confidential
process assumptions. Negative results are rarely published, while
high-fidelity measurements may arrive only after substantial engineering work.
These conditions change what counts as usable data. A paper may be sufficient
for retrieval or question answering and still omit the settings needed to rerun
the comparison or propose a valid change. When that context is missing, an
automated search can spend days evaluating a candidate that violates a known
constraint or targets the wrong workload.

Standardized benchmark suites such as SPEC CPU and MLPerf make scores
comparable by publishing workload versions, run rules, system configurations,
and reporting conventions [@SPEC2017CPU; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. Architecture
work needs comparable records for candidate provenance, simulator settings,
excluded workloads, and rejected alternatives.

@tbl-web-vs-arch-data compares the records commonly available for large-scale
model training with the records needed to act on an architecture design.

| **Dimension** | **Large text, code, or image corpora** | **Architecture design trajectories** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Unit of data | A document, code file, image, or labeled example can often stand on its own. | A useful record links design state, proposed action, anticipated effect, model prediction, tool observation, and decision. |
| Availability | Large public or licensed collections can be assembled. | Workloads, tool settings, reports, and review decisions are often private, fragmented, or discarded. |
| Required context | Local metadata and curation rules may be enough to interpret an example. | Workload, tool version, fidelity, constraints, provenance, and design version determine what an observation means. |
| Failed work | Noisy or low-quality examples may remain in a corpus. | Failed runs and rejected alternatives are often omitted even though they define where a design or proxy stopped working. |
| Acquisition cost | Existing public artifacts can be collected without rerunning the process that produced them. | New high-fidelity observations may require long simulations, implementation work, scarce tools, or hardware measurements. |

: **Architecture work depends on connected records.** Text, code, and images remain useful sources of knowledge, but AI-assisted design also requires versioned state, actions, observations, and decisions. {#tbl-web-vs-arch-data tbl-colwidths="[20,40,40]"}

The missing links are scattered across different places. Papers and
open-source tools expose some settings and artifacts, while commercial EDA
context and review decisions often remain private. Rejected work may be
discarded entirely. Public metadata can catalog what exists, but it cannot
recover simulator options, workload revisions, or decisions that were never
recorded. @sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources discusses how to catalog
and maintain those public resources.

ChipNeMo shows both the value and the limit of domain text. Hardware-design
material can improve a language model's performance on related tasks
[@LiuEtAl2023ChipNeMo], but that material does not reconstruct the trajectory
behind a specific architecture change.

## Record the Cost and Fidelity of Each Evaluation {#sec-sample-cost-is-architecture-data}

Architecture evaluations take different forms. A cycle-level simulation,
compiler report, failed synthesis run, power estimate, rejected floorplan,
expert review, and silicon measurement consume different resources and support
different conclusions. The record should link the prior state and proposed
action to the returned value or failure, then preserve the cost, fidelity,
provenance, coverage, and decision that followed.
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces explains how a run harness can
capture these fields when it invokes a tool.

These costs cannot be reduced to a single number. A simulation may consume
little compute but wait for a license; a prototype may depend on shared hardware
and days of engineering work. A post-layout result may be more realistic than
an analytical estimate, but it also arrives later and covers fewer candidates.
A field measurement may describe one deployed population and say little about
another. Recording these constraints separately helps an AI method choose an
evaluation it can actually run and helps an architect judge what the result can
support.

Concrete architecture tools span this range. Analytical mapping and dataflow
models are designed for broad design-space exploration
[@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop; @KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO]. Physical-design and
verification flows occupy the other end of the spectrum, where feedback can
take hours or days and the engineering effort belongs in the evaluation record
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign; @BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing; @Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy].
@tbl-sample-cost-regimes shows how the required context changes with the source
of feedback. Read each row from the time and resources needed to obtain an
observation, through what that observation can reveal, to the information a
later method or reviewer would need in order to reuse it.

| **Feedback source** | **Latency / cost intuition** | **What it reveals** | **Record for reuse** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Analytical model or mapper | Milliseconds to seconds; low direct cost; results depend heavily on model assumptions. | Supports pruning and sensitivity checks within the model's scope. | Model assumptions, workload slice, constraints, and proxy-validity notes. |
| Trace, profile, or replay | Seconds to hours depending on capture and replay setup. | Reveals workload behavior under a stated capture or replay policy. | Trace version, sampling policy, software stack, and filtering choices. |
| Cycle-level simulation | Minutes to days depending on model detail and target workload. | Returns a simulated observation scoped by abstraction, calibration, and unsupported states. | Simulator version, configuration, seeds, workload revision, and calibration notes. |
| RTL, gate-level, or EDA feedback | Hours to days for synthesis, timing closure, power analysis, or physical DRC/LVS. | Supplies scarce, multiobjective implementation observations. | Tool versions, constraints, process assumptions, waived warnings, and rejected candidates. |
| FPGA, emulation, or prototype | High setup and shared-resource cost; high throughput once mapped. | Higher speed changes observability and debugging semantics as well as wall-clock time. | Mapping constraints, observability limits, debug hooks, and queue/resource state. |
| Post-silicon bring-up or validation | Weeks to months and high acquisition cost. | Real hardware reveals integration, timing, power, reliability, and validation failures that earlier evidence missed. | Hardware stepping, firmware or microcode version, test context, failure mode, waiver, and owner. |
| Field or fleet telemetry | Months to years; deployment changes can complicate interpretation. | Real deployment shows workload drift, cohort effects, incidents, rollback triggers, and user-visible regressions. | Population, deployment version, sampling policy, privacy filter, incident context, and decision owner. |

: **Cost, fidelity, and scope change across evaluation paths.** The representation records the resources, assumptions, and context needed for a reviewer to judge what each observation can support. {#tbl-sample-cost-regimes tbl-colwidths="[20,24,24,22]"}

Cheap feedback supports broad screening but carries more model risk.
Implementation and deployment observations may support stronger claims after
review, yet only a small number of candidates can receive them. The
representation must preserve which evaluation path produced each observation
so that a proxy result is not mistaken for implementation evidence.

For tools like simulators, the timing side of this cost is multiplicative.
Simulation cost depends on the target cycles the workload demands and the rate
at which the simulator executes them. For a target clock
$f_{\mathrm{target}}$, workload duration $T_{\mathrm{workload}}$, and
simulation throughput $R_{\mathrm{sim}}$, the wall time is
$$
T_{\mathrm{wall}} =
\frac{N_{\mathrm{cycles}}}{R_{\mathrm{sim}}}
= \frac{f_{\mathrm{target}} \times T_{\mathrm{workload}}}{R_{\mathrm{sim}}}.
$$
@fig-simulation-wallclock plots this relationship for four illustrative
throughputs at a 1 GHz target. The shaded band marks runs that finish within one
day.

```{python}
#| label: fig-simulation-wallclock
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Simulation throughput limits how much target execution can be evaluated in a day.** Each line plots the illustrative wall-clock time $T_{\mathrm{wall}} = f_{\mathrm{target}} \times T_{\mathrm{workload}} / R_{\mathrm{sim}}$ for one simulator throughput at a 1 GHz target. The shaded band marks runs that finish within one day.
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-log chart of wall-clock simulation time versus target execution time at 1 gigahertz, with four parallel lines for simulator throughputs from 1 kilocycle per second to 100 megacycles per second and a shaded band marking runs that finish within one day. At 1 kilocycle per second only the 1 millisecond workload stays inside the band, at about 17 minutes, while one second of target time takes 11.6 days; at 100 megacycles per second even the one-hour workload finishes in about 10 hours."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

# Illustrative wall time = (f_target x T_workload) / R_sim for a 1 GHz target.
# No external dataset; every point is computed from the stated rates.
freq = 1e9
workloads = [("1 ms", 1e-3), ("1 s", 1.0), ("1 min", 60.0), ("1 h", 3600.0)]
rates = [
    ("1 kcycle/s", 1e3, COLORS["red"]),
    ("100 kcycle/s", 1e5, COLORS["orange"]),
    ("10 Mcycle/s", 1e7, COLORS["blue"]),
    ("100 Mcycle/s", 1e8, COLORS["green"]),
]
day = 86_400.0

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.6))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.15, right=0.78, top=0.95, bottom=0.17)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(5e-4, 7e3)
ax.set_ylim(2e-3, 3e10)

ax.axhspan(2e-3, day, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], zorder=0)
ax.axhline(day, color=COLORS["note_edge"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--", zorder=1)
ax.text(8e-4, day * 2.2, "one day", fontsize=5.8, color=COLORS["note_text"],
        fontweight="bold", va="bottom")
ax.text(2.6e-3, 4.5e-3, "finishes within one day", fontsize=5.8,
        color=COLORS["note_text"], fontweight="bold", va="bottom")

xs = [d for _, d in workloads]
for name, rate, color in rates:
    ys = [freq * d / rate for d in xs]
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=color, linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=14, color=color, zorder=3)
    ax.text(3600 * 1.4, ys[-1], name, va="center", fontsize=6.0, color=color,
            clip_on=False)

ax.set_xticks([1e-3, 1.0, 60.0, 3600.0])
ax.set_xticklabels(["1 ms", "1 s", "1 min", "1 h"])
ax.set_yticks([1.0, 60.0, 3600.0, day, 3.156e7, 3.156e9])
ax.set_yticklabels(["1 s", "1 min", "1 h", "1 day", "1 year", "100 years"])
ax.minorticks_off()
ax.set_xlabel("target execution time at 1 GHz", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("wall-clock simulation time", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```


At 1 kcycle/s in @fig-simulation-wallclock, simulating 1 ms of target execution
takes about 17 minutes, while 1 s takes about 11.6 days. At 100 Mcycle/s, even
1 hour of target execution takes about 10 hours. A faster simulator shifts the
whole line down, but the architect still has to choose among a shorter workload
slice, a faster model, and fewer high-fidelity evaluations. An AI method needs
those limits in the representation before it can plan which candidates to run.

Cost constrains search in more than one way. A co-design exercise with five
workload slices, four architecture configurations, and six compiler settings
has $5 \times 4 \times 6 = 120$ candidates. It may be practical to enumerate
them with an analytical model and impossible to do so with a physical-design
flow. Wall time, compute time, license capacity, access to shared hardware, and
engineering attention are separate constraints; one does not automatically
make up for another.

Learning-assisted chip placement makes the connection to representation
concrete. A placement evaluation is useful only when it remains linked to the
macros, nets, constraints, validity checks, and tool feedback that produced it
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. The same is true beyond placement. For
every expensive evaluation, the trajectory should record what it cost, what
part of the design space it covered, what it ruled out, and why the next action
was chosen.

@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization discusses methods for using
scarce evaluations efficiently. Those methods can distinguish useful
exploration from disconnected measurements only if every observation records
its cost, fidelity, and coverage.

## When Design State Is Left Implicit

Architecture projects rarely keep every relevant choice in one place. The
workload version may live in a benchmark directory, simulator options in a
script, an objective in a spreadsheet, and the reason for rejecting a candidate
in review slides or someone's memory. Experienced architects also carry
judgments that are easy to leave unstated, such as which proxy they distrust or
which integration risk they will not accept. This resembles the hidden
technical debt created by unmanaged data dependencies and assumptions in
software systems [@SculleyEtAl2015Hidden].

Architects and tool specialists working on the same project can sometimes fill
these gaps from shared context. An AI method should not be expected to guess.
If a constraint is missing, it may propose a change that the design cannot
accept. If a simulator option or workload revision is missing, another run may
not reproduce the result. If
rejected candidates are absent, a search may repeat known failures. If a plot
shows only the winner, another architect cannot tell why the alternatives were
discarded. The tool can complete successfully while the resulting comparison
is still wrong or impossible to review.

AlphaChip illustrates a related boundary. An organization may have private
training data and evaluation records that support an internal decision, while
outside readers can inspect only the released artifacts. The internal decision
and the public claim therefore do not rest on the same inspectable record.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The strongest training context remained private"}
**The claim.** AlphaChip was reported to place production macros at expert quality and to have
been used in production designs [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** Its strongest reported setup depended on proprietary production designs and
pretraining context that were not fully available to outside researchers.
Later reassessments could test released cases, baselines, and proxy metrics, but
could not replay that private context; the original authors replied that those
public reassessments had therefore not reproduced the published method
[@ChengEtAl2023MacroPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL;
@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** The public artifacts can support narrower evaluations, but they cannot make the
private setup independently replayable. Because private evidence and public
evidence support different claim boundaries, the evidence record must include
provenance, access restrictions, and the exact effect of redaction.
Restricted evidence may still support an internal decision, but the public
claim must stop where outside inspection stops.
:::

@sec-feedback-verification-trust revisits the broader reproducibility dispute.
For restricted evidence, @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives a concrete
redacted-card pattern. The organization making the internal decision should
retain the complete record under appropriate access controls and state
precisely what outside readers can verify.

Similar gaps appear across ordinary architecture artifacts.
@tbl-representation-debt identifies what each artifact contributes, what
context it often omits, and the failure that can follow. The representation
links these artifacts so an AI method can compare candidates and an architect
can review why one was accepted or rejected. A replay package separately
contains the runnable inputs and environment.

| **Artifact** | **What it enables** | **What it often hides** | **Failure mode** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Paper or plot | Claim, result, and comparison. | Tool flags, failed candidates, tuning history. | Reproduce only the reported result, not the design process. |
| Workload trace | Concrete input behavior and measurements. | Coverage, versioning, sampling policy, privacy filters. | Optimize for an unrepresentative slice. |
| Simulator config | Model settings. | Defaults, unsupported states, calibration limits. | Trust a number outside its valid scope. |
| RTL or EDA report | Implementation-facing feedback. | Process assumptions, constraints, waived warnings. | Accept an artifact that cannot close. |
| Review notes | Architect and reviewer judgment and rationale. | Unstated assumptions and discarded alternatives. | Lose why a decision was made. |
| Rejected candidate | Search boundary and negative evidence. | Why it failed and at what fidelity. | Rediscover known dead ends. |

: **Architecture artifacts become reusable when their context is recorded.** Each artifact should include enough provenance, assumptions, constraints, and validity information for a method to use it and for a reviewer to trace the result. {#tbl-representation-debt tbl-colwidths="[22,27,25,18]"}

No row in @tbl-representation-debt is sufficient alone. A workload trace needs
the simulator configuration that consumed it, and an EDA report needs the
candidate and constraints that produced it. Versioned links between the
artifacts turn a folder of files into a traceable design trajectory.

## What Paper-Based Benchmarks Can and Cannot Test

Public benchmarks present a different case. QuArch uses published papers to
test whether a model knows architectural concepts and can reason about reported
designs [@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch]. The later QuArch reasoning benchmark organizes
2,671 expert-validated questions around recall, analysis, design, and
implementation competencies [@PrakashEtAl2025QuArchReasoning]. These are useful
checks. A model that misunderstands basic architecture concepts is unlikely to
propose or critique a credible design change.

Paper-based questions do not test the whole task in this chapter. They do not
show whether an AI system can change a design under stated constraints, invoke
the right tools, or preserve the record another architect needs to review the
result. Published papers also rarely contain every simulator option, failed
candidate, and review decision from the original project.

@fig-quarch-boundary-test separates these two requirements. The upper layer
contains the concepts, published relationships, and paper-level reasoning that
QuArch can test. The lower layer connects a particular claim and design state
to proposed actions, tool observations, provenance, failed paths, review, and
decision.

![**QuArch tests paper knowledge.** A complete design record additionally links claim scope, represented state and actions, tool observations, provenance, failed paths, review, and decision.](images/F5b-quarch-boundary-test){#fig-quarch-boundary-test width="100%" fig-alt="Two-panel diagram showing a paper-knowledge layer with concept recall, published relationships, and paper-level reasoning separated from a design-trajectory layer linking claim scope, state, actions, tool observations, provenance, failed paths, review, and decision."}

The separation in @fig-quarch-boundary-test explains why both layers are
needed. Architectural knowledge can help an AI method propose or critique a
change, but it does not reveal which simulator options were used or why a
candidate was rejected. QuArch tests the knowledge layer; the linked trajectory
is what allows the method to act on a particular design and another architect
to inspect what happened.

## Provenance, Coverage, and Failed Alternatives {#sec-provenance-coverage-and-negative-traces}

Architecture projects often retain the accepted design and discard the work
that did not advance. That loses several different kinds of information. A tool
may crash before evaluating a candidate. A valid run may find no improvement.
A candidate may violate a constraint, or a proxy improvement may disappear at
higher fidelity. Each outcome should remain linked to the conditions under
which it occurred.

Compiler regression tests already treat failures this way. Once a compiler bug
becomes a regression test, later versions are checked against that specific
failure. Architecture projects should preserve rejected candidates for the same
reason, with enough provenance to determine whether an old failure still
applies.

An old failure does not automatically rule out a new candidate. The workload,
tool version, process assumption, or surrounding design may have changed. An AI
method can surface the earlier record and identify the conditions that match. A
declared review policy may specify when a failure must be reconsidered. The
architect or named reviewer then determines whether those conditions are met
and what check is needed before trying again.

When a trajectory contains only winners, an AI method or reviewer cannot see
which assumptions, tools, workloads, or regions of the design space were
already ruled out. Many proposed changes are invalid or misleading. A
generated RTL fragment may be syntactically plausible but fail timing or
violate an interface. A design-space search may find a candidate that looks
good under a proxy but fails under a better power model. A benchmark result may
improve because the workload slice is too narrow. A chiplet partition may
appear modular but introduce unacceptable latency, thermal coupling, test
complexity, or supply-chain risk.

Each of these failures should leave a record that identifies the candidate, the
condition it violated, and the resulting change to the next search or review
step. @tbl-negative-traces gives examples.

| **Failed run or rejected alternative** | **What it records** | **How it changes later work** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Synthesis or timing violation | Constraints, process, parameters, failing setup/hold paths. | Exclude this macro or parameter combination. |
| Unroutable or PDN failing netlist | Placement, density, pin layout, congestion map, IR drop. | Add congestion-aware spacing or reshape the action space. |
| Proxy win that fails fidelity | A cheap metric improves, but a higher-fidelity check overturns the apparent improvement. | Calibrate proxies and require sensitivity checks. |
| Tool failure or crash | Simulator, synthesis, compiler, or harness cannot complete. | Separate design failure from environment failure. |
| Coverage gap | Workload, input, scenario, or architecture class was not represented. | Mark the evidence boundary before committing. |
| Rejected design rationale | An architect or reviewer rejects a candidate for risk, maintainability, schedule, or integration. | Preserve the reason for later work and review. |

: **Rejected work remains useful when its conditions are recorded.** Failed builds, invalid candidates, missed constraints, and rejected alternatives can prevent repeated mistakes without turning one local failure into a universal rule. {#tbl-negative-traces tbl-colwidths="[26,32,32]"}

These records require enough provenance to interpret the environment that
produced the result. A replay claim additionally requires the runnable
environment and inputs. Without context, a later architect cannot tell whether
a failure still applies. The minimum reusable record names the candidate and
its context (workload slice, software stack, tool version, seed, parameters,
and constraints), the fidelity level, the failure reason, the coverage
boundary, and the decision owner. A failed run is reusable only when this
information also states which part of the design space, workload space, or
claim the failure applies to.

Keeping a failure record is worthwhile when it can save a future run, justify a
review decision, preserve a handoff, bound a public claim, or mark the condition
under which an old rejection should be reopened. Otherwise failure records
become either clutter or folklore, and the next architect or AI method cannot
tell whether a past rejection is still valid.

## When a Representation Can Support Action and Review

A representation is ready for action and review when it answers two practical
questions. Can the AI method identify the exact design version it may change,
the fields intended to vary, and the constraints that remain fixed? Can another
architect trace why the change was proposed, any model prediction used, the
tool observation, and the decision that followed?

The minimum record covers three stages.

- **Before the action:** the versioned design and workload, mutable fields,
  fixed constraints, proposed change, and anticipated effect.
- **During evaluation:** any model prediction, the declared check and its
  result, the tool path, returned observation or failure, units, cost, fidelity,
  provenance, coverage, and uncertainty.
- **After review:** the comparison, rejection reason when applicable, decision
  owner, decision, and resulting design state.

A replay package adds the runnable environment and inputs. The links matter
more than the file format. A score without units or workload provenance cannot
support a comparison. A candidate log without the declared mutable fields and
constraints cannot show where the method was allowed to act. A log that omits
rejected and invalid proposals cannot show what the method actually tried. A
record that keeps only the winner cannot help the next architect distinguish
an unexplored region from a known failure.

In the SCALE-Sim example described earlier, the record declared which geometry
fields could vary and which workload and simulator conditions remained fixed.
The tool environment applied the geometry checks before invoking SCALE-Sim and
recorded the result of each check.
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces develops that interface.

Before reusing a result for a new design change, check that the record connects
the candidate version and proposed action to the anticipated effect, any model
prediction, tool observation, cost, fidelity, provenance, and decision. A
missing link narrows what the result can support and may require recovering the
context or running a new check. A replay claim also requires the runnable
environment and inputs.

Keeping state, actions, observations, and decisions connected still leaves open
how to preserve useful records without making them stale, unwieldy, or unsafe
to share.

## Open Research Questions

The minimum record is clear, but five implementation questions remain. Each
concerns information an AI method needs in order to act and another architect
needs in order to review the result.

1. **How should an AI method express a proposed architecture change so tools and reviewers can check it?**
Informal proposals leave room for a model, simulator wrapper, and architect to
interpret the same change differently. A usable representation must show the
architecture question, the fields allowed to change, the fixed constraints,
the anticipated effect, and the required check. It must do so for work as
different as cache tuning, compiler scheduling, and physical design without
forcing every domain into the same schema. Invalid or ambiguous proposals also
need to be caught before they consume expensive tool time.

2. **Which parts of a multi-fidelity trajectory should an AI method retain?**
Keeping every intermediate file and log can make the record unusable, while
keeping only winners removes the evidence needed for later action. A cache
search may need rejected configurations and simulator settings, while an RTL or
EDA flow may need failing paths, constraints, and waived warnings. In both
cases, the AI method and reviewer need the links among prior state, action,
workload and tool provenance, observation cost and fidelity, and the decision
that followed. A retention policy must preserve those links without turning the
trajectory into an undifferentiated archive
(@sec-sample-cost-is-architecture-data).

3. **How can an AI method detect that a consequence model has moved beyond the conditions on which it was calibrated?**
New workloads, software stacks, tool versions, and design regions can invalidate
a predictor that was accurate on earlier candidates. The AI method needs
signals that trigger a higher-fidelity simulator or implementation check before
it relies on an unsupported prediction. Those signals must prevent false
acceptance without spending the full evidence budget on every candidate.

4. **What coverage information tells an AI method and reviewer whether a result transfers?**
Workload phases, trace slices, objectives, constraints, and observed failure
modes all affect whether an architectural relationship generalizes. A useful
coverage summary must expose both the evaluated region and the gaps. The AI
method and reviewer should be able to tell when a result applies, when its
support weakens, and when a new workload or design state requires fresh
evidence (@sec-toward-architecture-world-models).

5. **How should an AI method use failed runs without treating a local failure as a universal rule?**
A candidate may fail because of one process assumption, tool version, workload,
or parameter combination. Preserving the failure can prevent repeated mistakes,
but applying it too broadly may exclude valid designs. The rejection record
must name the conditions under which the failure occurred. The difficult part
is determining which recorded conditions actually caused the failure and which
changes are sufficient to justify trying the rejected alternative again
(@sec-provenance-coverage-and-negative-traces).

## Conclusion

Before an AI method can act, the representation must identify the exact design
and workload version, the fields intended to change, the constraints that
remain fixed, the proposed change, and its anticipated effect. It must also
retain any model prediction used to choose or justify that change. After a tool
run, the same record adds the returned observation or failure, units, cost,
fidelity, provenance, coverage, uncertainty, the decision, and the person
authorized to make it.
Those links let another architect trace the result back to what was proposed,
what actually ran, and why the design did or did not advance.

Linked, versioned trajectories preserve accepted and rejected work across
fidelity levels. A scoped consequence model separately estimates or constrains
what a proposed action may do within stated limits. An architecture world model
is the predictive case that maps represented state and action to a possible
next state or consequence. The tool environment in
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces checks whether the action can
run and records what the tool returns. A replay package adds the runnable inputs
and environment needed to repeat the work.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Keep the design version, proposed change, anticipated effect, model prediction, tool observation, cost, fidelity, provenance, coverage, failure, and decision connected.
- **Reader test:** Can an AI method tell exactly what it may change, and can another architect distinguish the prediction from the tool observation and trace the decision that followed?
- **Up next:** The representation records the design state, proposed actions, and observations. @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces explains how tool environments constrain those actions and produce interpretable tool results.
:::
